title: Worksheet 4: Making Decisions
description: Learn to use if, elif, and else statements to create programs that can make choices and respond to different conditions.

problems.title: True and False: Python's Yes/No Values
problems.content:
Python has two special yes/no values called booleans: True and False. You can print them just like numbers:

print(True)   → prints True
print(False)  → prints False

You can also print the result of comparing two numbers — Python will answer with True or False:

print(5 > 3)   → True  (5 is greater than 3 ✓)
print(2 > 9)   → False  (2 is not greater than 9 ✗)
problems.footer: The capital letter matters — Python knows True and False, but not true or false.

problems.title: Printing True and False
problems.content: Like numbers, you can print Python's boolean values directly. They always start with a capital letter.
problems.task: Run the code. Then add a third line that prints False.
problems.starterCode:
print(True)
print(True)
problems.hint: Add print(False) as a third line at the bottom.

problems.title: Fix the Typo: true vs True
problems.content: Python is picky about capital letters! True and False must always start with a capital. Using true (lowercase) causes an error.
problems.task: Run the code to see the error. Then fix the typo so the program prints True.
problems.starterCode: print(true)
problems.hint: Change true to True — Python needs the capital T.

problems.title: Comparisons give True or False
problems.content: Comparing two numbers also gives True or False. The > sign asks 'is the left number greater than the right?'
problems.task: Run the code. The first three lines print True, but the last one prints False. Change the numbers on the line print(4 > 9) to make it print True.
problems.starterCode:
print(8 > 3)
print(10 > 1)
print(7 > 2)
print(4 > 9)
problems.hint: Change the 9 in print(4 > 9) to any number less than 4.

rules.description: All four comparisons should print True

problems.title: Making Decisions: the if statement
problems.content:
So far, every line of your code always ran. The if statement changes that — it only runs the indented code when the condition is True.

Every if statement has two parts:

  • Condition — the True/False question between if and the colon
  • Body — the indented line(s) that only run when the condition is True

Python checks the condition. If it's True, it runs the body. If it's False, it skips the body entirely.

Notice the colon : at the end of the if line — just like loops in Worksheet 3.
problems.footer: Condition = the True/False question. Body = the code that runs when True.

problems.title: When the condition is True — body runs
problems.code:
n = 15
if n > 10:
  print('big number')
print('done')

problems.title: When the condition is False — body is skipped
problems.code:
n = 8
if n > 10:
  print('big number')
print('done')

problems.title: Greater Than >
problems.content: So far, our programs have always run the same way. But what if we want our code to make decisions? The if statement runs code only when a condition is true. We can use math symbols like > (greater than) to create these conditions.
problems.task: Run the code — age is 6, so age > 5 is True and 'OK' prints. Now change age to 3 and run again. Notice 'OK' no longer prints.
problems.hint: The code inside the if block only runs when the condition next to the if is True.

problems.title: Your Turn: Greater Than > Practice
problems.content: Now it's your turn to practice! This code starts with a False condition. Can you change the age to make the condition True and see 'OK' printed?
problems.task: The condition age > 5 is currently False. Change age to make the condition True so 'OK' gets printed.
problems.hint: Try changing age to 6 or any number greater than 5. Watch how the debug output changes to True!

problems.title: Less Than <
problems.content: Just like we use > for 'greater than', we can use < for 'less than'. Let's use our debug print to see how it works.
problems.task: Run the code — toys is 4, so toys < 5 is True and the message prints. Change toys to 5 and run again — what happens?
problems.hint: Try the number 5. Is 5 less than 5? The debug line will tell you!

problems.title: Your Turn: Less Than < Practice
problems.content: Time to practice the less than operator! This code starts with a False condition because 6 is not less than 5.
problems.task: The condition toys < 5 is currently False. Change toys to make the condition True so the message gets printed.
problems.hint: Try changing toys to 4 or any number less than 5. Watch the debug output change to True!

problems.title: Equal To ==
problems.content: This is a very important one! To ask if two things are equal, we use two equals signs: ==. A single equals sign = is for giving a value to a variable.
problems.task: Run the code — secret_number is 7, so secret_number == 7 is True and the message prints. Change secret_number to 6 and run again. Does it print?
problems.hint: Remember: one equals = gives a value, two equals == asks a question.

problems.title: Your Turn: Equal To == Practice
problems.content: Let's practice the equality operator! This code starts with a False condition because 5 does not equal 7.
problems.task: The condition secret_number == 7 is currently False. Change secret_number to the right value so the success message gets printed.
problems.hint: Try changing secret_number to 7. Remember, == checks if two values are exactly equal!

problems.title: Fix the Bug! (= vs ==)
problems.content: Oh no! A classic bug. This program uses a single = inside the if statement, which causes an error. This is one of the most common mistakes for new programmers!
problems.task: Run the code to see the error. Then, fix the bug by changing the single = to a double == so the program works correctly.
problems.hint: To ask a question inside an if statement, you need two equals signs: ==.

problems.title: Your Turn: Create a Number Check
problems.content: Great job fixing that bug! Now that you know the difference between = and ==, let's create a simple program from scratch.
problems.task: Write a program that sets a variable called number to 3, then uses an if statement to check if it equals 3. If it does, print 'yes'.
problems.starterCode: number = 3
problems.hint: Remember: use = to set the variable, and == to check if it equals 3. You only need an if statement and one print.

rules.description: Use an if statement to check if number equals 3: 'if number == 3:'
Your program should print 'yes' when number equals 3

problems.title: Two Paths: adding else
problems.content:
So far, when the condition is False, nothing happens. But what if we want to do something in that case too? We add an else block — it runs when the condition is False.

Python always goes down exactly one path — never both, never neither.
problems.footer: if = runs when True. else = runs when False. Only one ever runs.

problems.title: else in action — the False path runs
Adding an else
problems.content: What if we want to do something when the if condition is not true? We can add an else block. The code in the else block only runs if the if condition is False.
problems.task: Enter different numbers to see both paths. If you enter a number greater than 10, 'Big number' prints. For any other number, 'Small number' prints.
problems.hint: The if block and the else block are like two different paths. The program will only ever go down one of them.

problems.title: Your Turn: Add an else
problems.content: This program checks if a score is high, but does nothing when it's low. Let's fix that by adding an else block.
problems.task: Run the code with score = 3 — nothing prints! Add an else block so that when the score is not greater than 8, it prints Keep trying.
problems.hint: Add else: on the line after the if-block, then indent the next line and write your print statement.

rules.description: Print 'Keep trying' in the else block

problems.title: Two Buttons
problems.content: Let's use buttons instead of typing numbers! The function get_choice(2) shows two buttons. It gives back the number 1 for the first button and 2 for the second.
problems.task: Run the code. Click the first button. Then run it again and click the second button to see the different messages.
problems.hint: get_choice(2) pauses the program and waits for you to click a button.

problems.title: Your Turn: Choose Your Adventure
problems.content: Create an adventure game where players choose their path! Use get_choice(2) to let players pick between two options.
problems.task: Write a program that uses get_choice(2) to get the player's choice. If they choose 1, print 'Cave'. Add an else that prints 'Mountain' for any other choice.
problems.hint: Start with 'choice = get_choice(2)', then use 'if choice == 1:' and don't forget the 'else:' block.

rules.description: Use get_choice(2) to show two buttons for the player
Check if the choice equals 1: 'if choice == 1:'
Add an else block for the other choice

problems.title: More Than Two Paths: elif
problems.content:
elif means "else if" — it adds an extra condition to check when the one above it was False. You can have as many elifs as you need.

The order always goes: if first, then any elifs, then else last. else must always come after all the elifs — never before.

if condition1:    # checked first
  ...
elif condition2:  # checked if condition1 was False
  ...
elif condition3:  # checked if both above were False
  ...
else:             # runs if ALL conditions above were False
  ...

Python checks from top to bottom and runs the first block whose condition is True — then skips all the rest. Only one block ever runs.
problems.footer: if → elif → elif → else. elif comes before else, always.

problems.title: More Than Two Choices: elif
problems.content: What if we have more than two options? We can use elif, which is short for 'else if'. It lets us check another condition.
problems.task: Enter numbers like 2, 5, and 8. See how the program prints 'Low', 'Mid', or 'High' depending on the number.
problems.hint: The program checks each condition from top to bottom. As soon as one is true, it runs that code and skips the rest.

problems.title: Your Turn: Add an elif
problems.content: Let's practice by adding another choice to an existing program. This treasure hunt has three options, but we want to add a fourth!
problems.task: We've added a fourth treasure chest. First, change get_choice(3) to get_choice(4). Then, add a new elif that checks if the choice is 3 and prints 'Found a map'.
problems.hint: Your new elif choice == 3: block should go just before the final else:.

problems.title: All the Comparison Operators
problems.content:
You have used > and == already. Here are all six comparison operators Python understands — every one of them gives True or False:

OperatorMeaningExampleResult
>greater than8 > 3True
<less than2 < 7True
==equal to5 == 5True
!=not equal to4 != 9True
>=greater than or equal to5 >= 5True
<=less than or equal to3 <= 3True
problems.footer: != means 'not equal' — the ! is read as 'not'.

problems.title: Not Equal !=
problems.content: The != operator checks if two values are different. It's the opposite of ==.
problems.task: Click both buttons. Button 1 is the secret button — clicking anything else should trigger the != check. Run a few times to see both messages.
problems.hint: != means 'not equal to'. The body runs whenever choice is anything other than 1.

problems.title: Your Turn: Use !=
problems.content: Practice using != yourself.
problems.task: Write a program that uses get_choice(2). If the choice is not equal to 2, print Wrong button. Otherwise print Correct.
problems.hint: Your condition should be if choice != 2:.

problems.title: Greater Than or Equal To >=
problems.content: What if you want to include the number in your check? You can use >= which means 'greater than or equal to'. This problem will help you practice using >= specifically.
problems.task: Run the code with the score set to 50. It says 'Try again'. Change the > to >= so that a score of exactly 50 is also a pass. Keep the score as 50 to practice how >= works differently from >.
problems.hint: Just add an equals sign = after the greater than sign >. Notice how >= includes the boundary value (50) while > excludes it.

rules.description: Keep the score as 50 to practice the >= operator. Don't change the score value.
Use the >= operator in your if statement: 'if score >= 50:'
Don't change the score value. Keep it as 50 to learn how >= works.
Your code should print 'You passed' when the score is 50 and you use >=.

problems.title: The Drink Machine
problems.content: Your turn to build a program with multiple choices from scratch!
problems.task:
Write a program that uses get_choice(3) to simulate a drink machine.
If choice is 1, print 'Water'.
If choice is 2, print 'Juice'.
For any other choice, print 'Lemonade'.
problems.hint: You will need one if, one elif, and one else. Look at the code from the previous two problems for help.

problems.title: Quiz Question
problems.content: You can use decision-making to create a simple quiz. We can print a question and then use get_choice() to get the user's answer.
problems.task:
Write a program that asks a quiz question and provides three multiple-choice answers using get_choice(3).
The question is: "What is 10 + 5?"
The choices are: 1) 12, 2) 15, 3) 20.
Your program should print "Correct" for the right answer, and "Not quite" for the wrong ones.
problems.starterCode:
print("What is 10 + 5?")
print("1) 12")
print("2) 15")
print("3) 20")
problems.hint: The correct answer is choice 2. You can use an if choice == 2: block for the correct message and an else block for the incorrect message.

rules.solutionCode:
print("What is 10 + 5?")
print("1) 12")
print("2) 15")
print("3) 20")

choice = get_choice(3)
if choice == 2:
  print("Correct")
else:
  print("Not quite")

problems.title: Two Conditions with 'and'
problems.content: Sometimes you need to check if two things are true at the same time. For this, we can use the word and. The code inside the if will only run if the condition on the left AND the condition on the right are both true.
problems.task: Run this code. Try changing the variables. For the message to print, age must be 12 or more AND has_ticket must be True.
problems.starterCode:
age = 14
has_ticket = True

if age >= 12 and has_ticket == True:
  print("Welcome to the movie")
problems.hint: If you change age to 10, the first part is false, so the message won't print. If you change has_ticket to False, the second part is false, so it also won't print.

problems.title: Secret Password
problems.content: Let's use the and operator to check two inputs from the user.
problems.task: Write a program that asks for a number and a code. If the number is 7 AND the code is 123, print 'Access granted'. Otherwise, print 'Access denied'.
problems.starterCode:
num = get_input('num')
code = get_input('code')
problems.hint: Your if statement will look like this: if num == 7 and code == 123:.

problems.title: Magic Potions
problems.content: Decisions can change the value of variables! This is how games keep track of things like health or score. The line health = health + 5 takes the current value of health, adds 5, and then saves the result back into the health variable.
problems.task: Complete the program. Add an else block that subtracts 3 from health if the second choice is picked.
problems.starterCode:
health = 10
print("Your health is 10")
choice = get_choice(2)

if choice == 1:
  health = health + 5

print("Your health is now", health)
problems.hint: Add an else: block. Inside it, write the line health = health - 3.

rules.solutionCode:
health = 10
print("Your health is 10")
choice = get_choice(2)

if choice == 1:
  health = health + 5
else:
  health = health - 3

print("Your health is now", health)

problems.title: Key Concept: Indentation Inside a Loop
problems.content:
When you put an if inside a for loop, you need two levels of indentation. Each level adds 2 spaces:

for i in range(3):          # level 0
  if i == 1:               # level 1 — 2 spaces
    print(i)              # level 2 — 4 spaces

The if is indented because it's inside the loop. The print is indented again because it's inside both the loop and the if.
problems.footer: Rule: every time you go one level deeper, add 2 more spaces.

problems.title: Magic Potions: The Adventure
problems.content: Now let's make the potion game last for more than one round! By wrapping the code in a for loop, the player gets to make multiple decisions — this is how real games work.
problems.task: Complete the program so it plays 3 rounds. Each round, print the current health, get a choice, and update health. If choice is 1, add 5 to health. Otherwise, subtract 3. Print the final health at the end.
problems.starterCode:
health = 10
print("Starting health:", health)

for i in range(3):
  print("Your health is", health)
  choice = get_choice(2)
  

print("Final health:", health)
problems.hint: Inside the loop, after get_choice(2), add an if/else: if choice == 1 then health = health + 5, else health = health - 3.

problems.title: Finding the Remainder %
problems.content: In programming, there's a special math symbol called modulo, which is a percent sign %. It gives you the remainder of a division. For example, if you share 10 sweets between 3 friends, each gets 3 and there is 1 left over. So, 10 % 3 is 1.
problems.task: Run the code to see the remainder of 14 divided by 4. Change the numbers to see how it works with different values.
problems.starterCode: print(14 % 4)
problems.hint: 14 divided by 4 is 3 with a remainder of 2.

problems.title: Even or Odd?
problems.content: We can use the modulo operator % to see if a number is even or odd. Any even number can be divided by 2 with no remainder! So, if number % 2 is 0, the number is even.
problems.task: Enter different numbers, both even and odd, and run the code to see if the program gets it right.
problems.hint: Try entering 10, then try 5. See how the result of number % 2 changes the outcome.

problems.title: Loop with Modulo
problems.content: Let's combine loops from Worksheet 3 with the modulo operator we just learned! This program loops through numbers from 1 to 20 and uses an if statement to check which ones are multiples of 3.
problems.task: Run the code to see it print all the multiples of 3. Then, change the code to print the multiples of 4 instead.
problems.hint: You only need to change one number in the if statement.

problems.title: Challenge: Dance Party
problems.content: Now that you've practiced loops and decisions, let's combine them to create an interactive dance sequence!
problems.task: Write a program that uses a for loop to run 4 times. Inside the loop, use get_choice(3) to pick a dance move ('Spin', 'Jump', or 'Wiggle') and print a message for each move.
problems.hint: Use for i in range(4):. Inside that loop, get a choice and use an if/elif/else structure to print the chosen move.

problems.title: Challenge: FizzBuzz Intro
problems.content: This is a classic programming challenge! The goal is to loop through numbers and print 'Fizz' for multiples of 3. It's a great way to practice loops and the modulo operator.
problems.task: Write a program that loops from 1 to 20. Inside the loop, if a number is a multiple of 3, print 'Fizz'. Otherwise, just print the number itself.
problems.hint: Use a for loop and an if/else statement. The condition to check is i % 3 == 0.

problems.title: Creative Challenge
problems.content: You've learned about variables, loops, and now decisions! These are the three main building blocks of almost every program. Now it's your turn to combine them in a creative way.
problems.task: Create your own interesting program. It could be a simple game, an interactive story, or a cool calculator. Use at least one variable, one loop, and one if statement. Be creative!
problems.hint: You could make a guessing game with limited lives, a story where choices affect your score, or a program that asks for a number and then draws a pattern of that size.
